title: Apply: The Bill of Rights
description: Apply your understanding of the adoption of and content within the Bill of Rights with this set of multiple-choice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article in the *Virginia Independent Chronicle* (1788).**

Our rights as a free people, were fixed and fully expressed at the head of our different constitutions. Why then should they not be placed also [[☃ definition 1]] of this general federation, which may [[☃ definition 2]] these? Some states, it is true, have no bill of rights, but it is an additional reason for inserting one in the federal [constitution]; since as Americans their rights are the same, and making a greater [[☃ definition 3]] of those rights to the federal body, that precaution is at present [[☃ definition 4]]: it is not possible now to be ignorant of our rights, and to contest them, being so near of the glorious [[☃ definition 5]] of their [[☃ definition 6]]; but it will be attempted in the future time and the history of all ages announce that they will no more be spared, than those of all the people who have preceded us, if we have no more care for their preservation.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: that is, in the federal constitution
the act of granting something to someone else

choices.content: The presence of a bill of rights in state constitutions makes a federal bill of rights unnecessary.
The inclusion of specific rights in the federal constitution will limit what rights the federal government protects.
The addition of a federal bill of rights is necessary to ensure that individual rights and liberties are protected.
A bill of rights is necessary only to make sure that state governments do not violate individual rights.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article in the *Virginia Independent Chronicle* (1788).**

Our rights as a free people, were fixed and fully expressed at the head of our different constitutions. Why then should they not be placed also [[☃ definition 1]] of this general federation, which may [[☃ definition 2]] these? Some states, it is true, have no bill of rights, but it is an additional reason for inserting one in the federal [constitution]; since as Americans their rights are the same, and making a greater [[☃ definition 3]] of those rights to the federal body, that precaution is at present [[☃ definition 4]]: it is not possible now to be ignorant of our rights, and to contest them, being so near of the glorious [[☃ definition 5]] of their [[☃ definition 6]]; but it will be attempted in the future time and the history of all ages announce that they will no more be spared, than those of all the people who have preceded us, if we have no more care for their preservation.

**Which group would most likely agree with the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: that is, in the federal constitution
the act of granting something to someone else

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *Observations upon the Proposed Plan of Federal Government* (1788).**

When Independence was declared by the Americans, they had no government to [control] them: Were free to [choose] the form most agreeable to themselves. Six of the States have no Bill of Rights; wisely judging, that such declarations tend to [[☃ definition 1]], rather than preserve their liberties. They considered their Constitutions as the evidence of the social compact between the governors and the governed, and the only proof of the rights yielded to the former. In all disputes respecting the exercise of power, the Constitution or frame of government decides. 

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The state constitutions do not protect individual rights and liberties, making a federal bill of rights necessary.
The amendments in a bill of rights need to be written in a way that protects individual rights on both the federal and state levels.
The federal bill of rights will override any state bill of rights due to the Supremacy Clause in the Constitution.
The inclusion of specific rights in the federal constitution will limit what rights the federal government protects.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *Observations upon the Proposed Plan of Federal Government* (1788).**

When Independence was declared by the Americans, they had no government to [control] them: Were free to [choose] the form most agreeable to themselves. Six of the States have no Bill of Rights; wisely judging, that such declarations tend to [[☃ definition 1]], rather than preserve their liberties. They considered their Constitutions as the evidence of the social compact between the governors and the governed, and the only proof of the rights yielded to the former. In all disputes respecting the exercise of power, the Constitution or frame of government decides. 

**Which group would most likely agree with the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from the Virginia Declaration of Rights (1776).**

That general warrants, whereby an officer or messenger may be commanded to search suspected places without evidence of a [[☃ definition 1]] committed, or to seize any person or persons not named, or whose offense is not [[☃ definition 2]] described and supported by evidence, are grievous and oppressive and ought not to be granted.

**This excerpt inspired which amendment in the Bill of Rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt from the Virginia Declaration of Rights (1776).**

That a well regulated militia, composed of the body of the people, trained to arms, is the proper, natural, and safe defense of a free state; that standing armies, in time of peace, should be avoided as dangerous to liberty; and that, in all cases, the military should be under strict [[☃ definition 1]] to, and be governed by, the [[☃ definition 2]] power. . . .

**This excerpt inspired which amendment in the Bill of Rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: placement under the power of another
connected with the general government, not the military

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the September 12, 1787 notes at the Constitutional Convention.**

Williamson and Gerry both [[☃ definition 1]] that the Constitution didn’t guarantee jury trials in civil cases, which led Mason to say that the Constitution needed a Bill of Rights, and he would be willing to second a motion to that purpose.

Gerry provided the motion and Mason seconded.

Sherman thought the motion was unnecessary. The states’ constitutions had bills of rights, and the U.S. Constitution had no authority to overturn those. Mason was not [[☃ definition 2]] by this, rightly noting, “The laws of the United States are to be [[☃ definition 3]] to State Bills of Rights.”

The motion for a Bill of Rights was unanimously rejected.

**This excerpt best serves as evidence of which fact about the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The delegates worried that adding a bill of rights would undermine the Constitution.
The delegates encouraged state legislatures to pass their own bills of rights.
The delegates wanted the U.S. Constitution to overturn state constitutions.
The delegates did not agree on whether to include a bill of rights in the U.S. Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Virginia Declaration of Rights (1776).**

That excessive bail ought not to be required, nor excessive fines imposed; nor cruel and unusual punishments inflicted.

**This excerpt inspired which amendment in the Bill of Rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**This is an excerpt of an article published in 1787 in Philadelphia’s *Independent Gazetteer* under the [[☃ definition 1]] An Old Whig IV.**

Men when they enter into society, yield up a part of their natural liberty, for the sake of being protected by government. If they yield up all their natural rights they are absolute slaves to their governors. If they yield up less than is necessary, the government is so [[☃ definition 5]], that it cannot protect them.—To yield up so much, as is necessary for the purposes of government; and to [[☃ definition 6]] all beyond what is necessary, is the great point, which ought, if possible, to be attained in the formation of a constitution. . . . 

To define what portion of his natural liberty, the subject shall at all times be entitled to retain, is one great [[☃ definition 2]] of a bill of rights. To these may be added in a bill of rights some particular engagements of protection, on the part of government, without such a bill of rights, firmly securing the privileges of the [[☃ definition 3]], the government is always in danger of [[☃ definition 4]] into tyranny; for it is certainly true, that “in establishing the powers of government, the rulers are invested with every right and authority, which is not in explicit terms reserved.”

**Which group would most likely agree with the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: declining; passing into a worse state

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt of an article published in 1787 in Philadelphia’s *Independent Gazetteer* under the [[☃ definition 1]] An Old Whig IV.**

Men when they enter into society, yield up a part of their natural liberty, for the sake of being protected by government. If they yield up all their natural rights they are absolute slaves to their governors. If they yield up less than is necessary, the government is so [[☃ definition 5]], that it cannot protect them.—To yield up so much, as is necessary for the purposes of government; and to [[☃ definition 6]] all beyond what is necessary, is the great point, which ought, if possible, to be attained in the formation of a constitution. . . . 

To define what portion of his natural liberty, the subject shall at all times be entitled to retain, is one great [[☃ definition 2]] of a bill of rights. To these may be added in a bill of rights some particular engagements of protection, on the part of government, without such a bill of rights, firmly securing the privileges of the [[☃ definition 3]], the government is always in danger of [[☃ definition 4]] into tyranny; for it is certainly true, that “in establishing the powers of government, the rulers are invested with every right and authority, which is not in explicit terms reserved.”

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: declining; passing into a worse state

choices.content: Citizens must retain all of their natural rights for a government to remain free and avoid descending into tyranny.
Citizens do not need a bill of rights to protect their individual rights because the federal government’s power is limited under the Constitution.
Citizens need a bill of rights to prevent an oppressive government and ensure that certain rights are secured against government overreach.
Citizens need to give up some rights in exchange for protection from a federal government, meaning that a bill of rights makes government ineffective.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article published in 1787 in the *Massachusetts Centinel* under the [[☃ definition 1]] Valerius.**

Now will any man come forward and say, that the Congress under the new Constitution will have a single power to limit the operation of this essential privilege; and if they have in what passage is such a power expressed? We have declared in this State, that the liberty of the press is an [[☃ definition 2]] right of the people—Can the Congress [[☃ definition 3]] this right? The moment they attempt it the new Constitution would be [[☃ definition 4]] and the question would be put on the issue of force.—Our State Constitution has declared that each member of society is possessed of certain natural rights, privileges and immunities.—Does the Federal Constitution say otherwise?—No—It is set up merely to [[☃ definition 5]] them.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution must give Congress more authority over the states, as state governments can still violate people’s natural rights.
Each state must add a bill of rights to their constitution, as only rights specifically listed in state constitutions are protected against federal interference.
The only way to guarantee the federal government cannot violate civil liberties is to add a Bill of Rights to the Constitution that resembles states’ bills of rights.
Each state has its own bill of rights listing which rights are protected, making a federal bill of rights unnecessary.

The moment they attempt it the new Constitution would be [[☃ definition 4]] and the question would be put on the issue of force.—Our State Constitution has declared that each member of society is possessed of certain natural rights, privileges and immunities.—Does the Federal Constitution say otherwise?—No—It is set up merely to [[☃ definition 5]] them.

**Which group would most likely agree with the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
